titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Ellen Spender, 13 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spenderr (1876 - 1960) to his mother, Ellen.
Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1//157A-B

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
<Edel
Mab
Rex
Lily
Maters Nov 13 My dear Mater. As Lily tells me that she will be staying
with you in the ever hospitable old home I shall write this
to you & let you pass it on to her by way of a change! I am glad to think she is under your & Jessie's kind care whilst
she makes up her mind as to the future plans about which I
am longing to hear. My news is nil. We are going on in much the old
routine at present, & in the kind of weather we are having
must consider ourselves lucky in being in such comfortable
quarters instead of wallowing in deep mud further forward, but
we shall doubtless take our turn at the mud soon & will
then wish for spring as much as any small bird or beastie
in the field. My usual day's routine at present is rather monoton-
ous. Every letter that comes to this office for one of the 14 staff
officers I see, & split up into the right sections of the staff,
being responsible that Russell the Chief of the Staff is made
aware of all important decisions, & that all the staff are
working on the same lines. Letters & telegrams come in at
all hours of the day or night - the largest lot about 10 pm
but those which come between 11 pm & 7 am are shown to
the officer on night duty & I only mark them out at 7.30
on my way to breakfast.
In some ways there is not more to do than there was in
Belfast, in fact except on occasions there is sometimes less though
when we move or are in engaging the enemy there is much more
of course. Now we have 14 18 officers to deal with what we
then had only 4 or even only 3 sometimes to deal with.
The Staff of the Division is as follows:
General Oliver Nugunt
General Staff 1 Lt Colonel Hon A Russell Responsible for general
direction of Division, ie
" " 2 Capt W B Spender } all plans of operations,
movements of troops,
" " 3 " Richardson intelligence, censoring, etc.
Administrative Staff
AAQMG Lt Col Comyn Responsible for Discipline
Billets & supplies of food
DAAQMG Major Singleton } forage horses ammunition
DAQMG Capt Mayne & waggons.
ADC 1 Lord Farnham Responsible for Camp &
Quarters of H Q rs & for Mess etc
ADC 2 Lt Henry Bars Horses of staff
servants
ADMS Lt Col Craig Principal Medical Officer
DADMS Major Boylan Smith Sanitary officer who
inspects all Sanitary arrangements
in billets
DADOS Capt Brummell Supply of clothing ammunit
ion & blankets
DADRS Major Webb Principal Veterinary officer
of the 5500 horses we have
Asst Provost Marshal Capt Appleton Deals with police & prisoners
& French offenders.
Claims Officer Capt Hon O'Neill MP deals with payment for
damage & claims of French inhabit
ants.
French liaison off Captain Duc de Luynes Arranges things with the (he has now left) French Mayors
French Interpreter Lt Beamish Head of the interpreters I am usually in during the morning unless I go out
on a Divisional day with the General or inspect one of the
bombing schools when our Grenadiers are trained. During this
time I get the correspondence arranged & afterwards as now
write letters, which are interrupted every few minutes by callers
& messages, & which are in consequence very disconnected.
This is I think the worst of this kind of life. From 7 AM till
11 pm one is never off duty, & even at meal times messages
continually come; one never gets 10 consecutive minutes to oneself
nor except when one is out for exercise any time alone. However
I do not mean to grumble as there must be thousands now
who would envy my life, & my own 2 room companions
Russell and Richardson are both very nice indeed, tho' I find some
of the others hard to get on with. The French houses we are in are all rather dilapidated, not
due to the Germans who passed through too quickly to do much
damage, but because the French (quite rightly) have no word
home , nor any idea of it. My own bedroom is uncommonly
nice compared to most but , being over an arch & facing North is
rather damp, but my bed is comfortable, & that is the main
thing, whilst Douglas can keep the boards clean. The old
dame is very suspicious of the 2 men but on the whole gets
on with them quite well & I fancy they give her a good deal
more in the way of help than they get, but she watches
like a cat to see that they don't touch any of her possessions
coal, wood, etc. The new Cabinet war Council is out — mostly old men
& none of them master minds —. Perhaps this means less interfer-
ence with the Military & Naval officers in the conduct of affairs, & if
these are true good men & g true — not political job hunters — it maybe
all right. I don't see K coming back to the new organisation
though, & I expect he will direct operations in the East for
some time, & then I hope come here. I am anxious to hear both your news of how
you think each other are looking. Best of love
Your loving son Wilfrid B. Spender No mail came yesterday nor today. I hope for several letters tonight mine may be delayed.
